questions.question: Two forces act on a point in opposite directions. Force A is $15\text{ N}$ to the right and Force B is $20\text{ N}$ to the left. What is the resultant force?
questions.explanation:
Taking right as positive: $(+15\text{ N}) + (-20\text{ N}) = -5\text{ N}$, which means $5\text{ N}$ to the left. 

**Common mistake:** Adding magnitudes to get $35\text{ N}$ without accounting for opposite directions.

questions.question: Two forces, one of $15\text{ N}$ and one of $20\text{ N}$, act at right angles to each other on a box. Calculate the magnitude of the resultant force.
questions.explanation:
Using Pythagoras' theorem: $R = sqrt{15^2 + 20^2} = sqrt{225 + 400} = sqrt{625} = 25\text{ N}$. 

**Common mistake:** Simply adding the two values to get $35\text{ N}$, which only works if they are parallel.

questions.question: A runner moves $5\text{ km}$ North and then $7\text{ km}$ South. What are her total distance and final displacement?
questions.explanation:
Distance is scalar (total path): $5 + 7 = 12\text{ km}$. Displacement is vector (change in position): $+5\text{ (N)} + -7\text{ (S)} = -2\text{ km}$, or $2\text{ km}$ South. 

**Common mistake:** Confusing distance (scalar) with displacement (vector) and giving the same value for both.

questions.question: When using the parallelogram method to find the resultant of two vectors starting from the same point, what represents the resultant vector?
options: The diagonal of the parallelogram from the point of origin
The sum of the lengths of all four sides
The shorter side of the parallelogram
questions.explanation:
In the parallelogram method, the resultant is represented by the diagonal starting from the origin point of the two vectors. 

**Common mistake:** Measuring a side of the parallelogram instead of the diagonal.

questions.question: Two forces $F_1 = 4\text{ N}$ (horizontal) and $F_2 = 3\text{ N}$ (vertical) act on an object. What is the angle $theta$ of the resultant force relative to the horizontal force?
questions.explanation:
Using trigonometry: $tan theta = Opposite / Adjacent = 3 / 4$. $theta = arctan(0.75) = 36.9^{circ}$. 

**Common mistake:** Swapping the opposite and adjacent sides, leading to an incorrect angle of $53.1^{circ}$.
